Baby Names: Naming Trends Through Time

24693197

Introduction

  • Used US baby name data (1910-2014, by state) alongside Billboard chart history and HBO titles/credits to look at naming trends

Naming Persistence Over Time

For each year, I took the top 25 boys’ and girls’ names nationally (summed across states), and compared that ranking to the top 25 from the same sex three years later using Spearman rank correlation (only names appearing in both years are compared).

  • Persistence has become noticably more volatile for both sexes since roughly the 1960s
    • 1990 falls in the middle of this already-volatile period rather than marking its start
  • 1990 is not the clear turning point the agency suspected because girls’ names began losing persistence as early as the 1950s/60s (whereafter it experienced a sharp dip), decades before 1990
  • Girls’ names show a sharp uptick in the final years of the series
    • It’s too early in the data to call this a lasting reversal rather than one more swing in an already volatile pattern
  • Implication for the agency: a name’s current popularity is now a less reliable predictor of its popularity several years later, so committing early to a single trending name carries more risk than it once did

Naming Surges

I looked for names with the biggest single-year jump in national popularity.

  • Several of the biggest male-name surges (Robert, John, James, Richard, Michael) all happen in exactly the same year (1946) which suggests something bigger was driving names generally that year, not a separate cause for each one
    • This is likely due to the post-WW1 baby-boom that occurred
  • Female surges (Linda, Shirley, Ashley) are more spread out across different years, more consistent with separate, name-specific causes
    • Shirley’s surge coincides with the rise of child actress Shirley Temple, who became one of Hollywood’s top stars by 1934 and was the industry’s #1 box-office draw from 1935-1938, which is a classic example of a single celebrity driving a national naming trend.
    • Linda and Ashley most likely follow a similar culturally-driven trend as Shirley

More Recent Rising Names

  • 2005 to 2014’s fastest-rising names (Sophia, Harper, Liam, Mason, etc.) show steady multi-year growth rather than a single sharp spike
  • Daleyza stands out as a name with zero history before 2012, then immediate growth (a different pattern from names simply gaining popularity over time)
    • Interestingly, a quick google search attributes this rise in the name to the Mexican-American singer (Larry Hernandez) naming his daughter in 2010

Conclusions for the Agency

  • Persistence has become less predictable since the 1960s, not specifically since 1990 as suspected - both boys’ and girls’ rankings show clear volatility decades before 1990, so the 1990s aren’t a clean turning point, but the broader instinct (recent name trends are less stable than older ones) holds for the second half of the dataset
  • Practical implication for toy naming: a name sitting in the top 25 today is a weaker guarantee of future popularity than it would have been pre-1960s so betting heavily on one trending name for a toy line carries more risk now than in earlier decades
  • Large naming surges have two distinct flavours, and they call for different agency responses:
    • Population-wide events (e.g. the 1946 male-name cluster, likely tied to the post-WWII baby boom) lift already-popular names rather than creating new fashionable ones - not useful for spotting a “next big name,” since nothing new is actually trending
    • Culturally-driven, name-specific spikes (Shirley Temple, likely Daleyza/Larry Hernandez) are the ones actually worth watching - these show a single celebrity, character, or cultural moment can drive rapid naming adoption, which is the more actionable pattern for predicting future toy-name candidates
  • Watch for “zero-to-spike” names, not just “rising” names: Daleyza’s pattern (no history, then sudden appearance) is a meaningfully different signal from gradually-rising names like Sophia or Liam - a sudden zero-to-spike name is more likely tied to a specific, identifiable cultural trigger, which is more useful for the agency’s stated goal of tying toy names to cultural moments
  • Caveat: this analysis focused on the baby-names data itself; a deeper dive cross-referencing HBO character names and Billboard artists/songs against naming spikes (as outlined in the brief) was not completed in this iteration, but the existing surge-detection method (find_name_surges()) is already set up to support that extension
